english: Pork liver and salted vegetable soup

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Singaporean-style pork liver soup with salted vegetable and spinach; closest match is pork liver soup (FOODON_03315595) used as approximate anchor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pig liver, cooked — used as closest FoodEx2 anchor for pork liver soup; soup matrix not separately coded in FoodEx2 at this specificity.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for pork liver soup as a composite dish; A0CKP (pig liver, cooked) used as the primary ingredient anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for pork liver soup as a composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of pork liver soup is approximately 350 g (one bowl), as commonly consumed in a Singaporean hawker or home-cooked meal context.

nutrient_source.title: Pork Liver Soup — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Pork Liver Soup (F06100030), which matches the dish description: soup with pork liver, salted vegetable, spring onion and spinach.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singaporean pork liver soup. GI estimated at approximately 32 (low) by analogy with clear meat-based broths and soups with negligible carbohydrate content (carbohydrate_g = 0.3 per 100g). Glycemic load per 350g serving = (32 × 0.3 × 3.5) / 100 = 0.3, confirming negligible glycaemic impact. Reference: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content at 290 mg per 100 g; a 350 g serving delivers approximately 1,015 mg sodium, representing 44% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit portion size to 200 ml of broth and request reduced-sodium soy sauce preparation, as the sodium load from a full bowl can approach half the recommended daily intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (0.3 g per 100 g) and high protein (5.8 g per 100 g) result in negligible glycaemic impact and a glycaemic load of approximately 0.3 per serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Pork liver soup is a low-GI, low-carbohydrate option suitable for blood glucose management in type 2 diabetes, provided it is consumed without added starch thickeners and as part of a balanced meal with high-fibre accompaniments.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Pork liver is a cholesterol-rich organ meat; while total fat is low (1.4 g per 100 g), the sodium content (290 mg per 100 g) and organ-meat cholesterol load are relevant for cardiovascular risk management.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption frequency of pork liver soup to once or twice per week, request low-sodium preparation, and avoid drinking the full volume of broth to reduce cumulative sodium and dietary cholesterol intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pork liver soup (猪肝汤, Zhu Gan Tang) is a traditional Singaporean Chinese clear broth soup made by briefly simmering thinly sliced pork liver with salted vegetable (kiam chye), spring onion, and spinach in a light stock seasoned with soy sauce, sesame oil, and ginger. The soup is characterised by a savoury, mildly salty broth with a clean, iron-rich flavour from the liver. Pork liver is tender when correctly cooked — sliced thin and added to boiling broth for only 1–2 minutes to prevent toughening. The dish is a staple in Singaporean hawker centres and home kitchens, commonly consumed as a side soup alongside rice or porridge. Nutritionally, it is low in calories (37 kcal per 100 g), high in protein (5.8 g per 100 g), and a notable source of dietary iron (2.1 mg per 100 g), making it valued for iron repletion and post-partum recovery in traditional Chinese dietary practice. Sodium is elevated at 290 mg per 100 g due to salted vegetable and soy sauce, which is a key consideration for hypertension management. Carbohydrate content is negligible (0.3 g per 100 g), resulting in a very low glycaemic load suitable for type 2 diabetes. Pork liver is an organ meat rich in vitamin A, B12, and folate, though detailed micronutrient values beyond those reported by HPB are not available in the current record. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those following halal or kosher diets.
common_user_queries: How many calories in pork liver soup?
Is pork liver soup good for diabetics?
Can I eat pork liver soup with high blood pressure?
meal_context: served alongside steamed rice or congee

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F06100030), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. The record is an exact match to the dish description. Confidence is set to high per Project Veritas source policy.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available from HPB source): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These detailed fatty acid, micronutrient, and moisture values are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this dish as of 2026-04-16. Saturated_fat_g estimated at 0.4 g per 100 g based on USDA FoodData Central pork liver cooked values (NDB 10397) proportionally adjusted for the dilute soup matrix; this is an approximation and should be verified if a direct HPB value becomes available. GI is an analogous estimate (low, ~32) based on the negligible carbohydrate content of the soup; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Input dietary_tags ('vegetarian', 'plant_based', 'keto') were removed as they are factually incorrect for a pork liver dish; 'soft_foods' and 'low_calorie' tags were retained in classification context. Input health_suitability condition key 'diabetes' has been updated to 'type2_diabetes' per Veritas naming rules.
